title: Net Effective Rent: What It Is and Why It Matters
metaTitle: Net Effective Rent Explained: The Real Cost of a Commercial Lease | Your Office Space
metaDescription: Net effective rent is the true cost of a commercial lease after incentives. Learn how to calculate it and why face rent comparisons are misleading.
excerpt: Face rent is the number landlords advertise. Net effective rent is what you actually pay. The difference can be significant — here's how to calculate it.
author: Joe Kelley
body:
## What is net effective rent?

Net effective rent (NER) is the actual average cost of a commercial lease after all incentives — rent-free periods, fitout contributions, and other landlord concessions — are factored in. It's the number that matters when comparing leases. Face rent, the headline number a landlord advertises, tells you very little without it.

## Face rent vs net effective rent

Face rent is what's on the sign. It's what the landlord's agent leads with. It's also almost never what you actually pay — at least not for the full term.

Commercial leases routinely include incentives: rent-free periods at the start, fitout contributions paid by the landlord, or periods of reduced rent. These incentives reduce the real cost of the lease below the face rent figure.

Net effective rent adjusts for those incentives and gives you a single, comparable number.

## How to calculate net effective rent

The formula is straightforward:

**NER = (Total rent payable over the term − Total incentives) ÷ Lease term in years ÷ Floor area**

Let's work through an example.

### Example

- Floor area: 500 sqm
- Face rent: $400/sqm/year
- Lease term: 5 years
- Rent-free period: 3 months
- Fitout contribution from landlord: $50,000

**Step 1: Total face rent over term**
500 sqm × $400 × 5 years = $1,000,000

**Step 2: Value of rent-free period**
3 months = 0.25 years × 500 sqm × $400 = $50,000

**Step 3: Total incentives**
$50,000 (rent-free) + $50,000 (fitout contribution) = $100,000

**Step 4: Total rent actually paid**
$1,000,000 − $100,000 = $900,000

**Step 5: NER**
$900,000 ÷ 5 years ÷ 500 sqm = **$360/sqm/year**

The face rent is $400/sqm. The net effective rent is $360/sqm. That's a 10% difference — and it matters when you're comparing two or more leases.

## Why comparing on face rent alone is misleading

Imagine you're choosing between two spaces:

| | Space A | Space B |
|---|---|---|
| Face rent | $400/sqm | $380/sqm |
| Lease term | 5 years | 5 years |
| Floor area | 500 sqm | 500 sqm |
| Rent-free | 3 months | 0 months |
| Fitout contribution | $50,000 | $0 |
| **Net effective rent** | **$360/sqm** | **$380/sqm** |

Space A has a higher face rent but a lower net effective rent. If you compare on face rent, you pick the wrong space.

This happens in real negotiations. Tenants are shown the headline number and assume it's comparable. It often isn't.

## What counts as an incentive?

Incentives that reduce NER include:

- **Rent-free periods** — the most common form
- **Landlord fitout contributions** — cash or works provided by the landlord
- **Reduced rent periods** — sometimes called a rent abatement or stepped rent structure
- **Parking provided at no cost** — can reduce your occupancy cost even if it doesn't reduce face rent directly
- **Relocation allowances** — less common but negotiable in large deals

## What NER doesn't account for

NER is a useful comparison tool but it has limits. It doesn't account for:

- **Outgoings** — some leases are gross (outgoings included), others are net (you pay outgoings on top). Always clarify this.
- **Rent review mechanisms** — a lease with uncapped market reviews can escalate significantly
- **Make-good costs** — your exit cost isn't included in NER
- **Quality of the space** — a lower NER in a C-grade building may still be a worse deal than a higher NER in an A-grade building

## Why this matters in practice

Landlord's agents know most tenants don't calculate NER. They present face rent confidently because it sounds lower than it would after factoring in what other landlords are offering in incentives.

When you know NER, you can compare leases properly. You can also tell whether the incentives on offer are reasonable for current market conditions — or whether you should be asking for more.
